free_text: Measurement of the 10B(alpha,n0) 13N cross section
for 2.2 < Ea < 4.9 MeV and its application as a
diagnostic at the National Ignition Facility
Differential cross sections were normalized to data
in REL-REF at 90 deg.
Measurements were performed using a
5MV single ended NEC Pelletron accelerator at the
University of Notre Dame Nuclear Science Laboratory.
Targets were prepared by electron-
gun sputtering a thin layer of enriched 10B powder
onto a 0.5-mm-thick Tantalum backing. Mean thickness
of 10B layer was (13+-1)mu-g/cm2
Neutron detection system consisted of five
deuterated liquid scintillator detectors, one of type
EJ-301D (size: 7.6-cm-thick x 7.6-cm diameter), one
of type EJ-315 (size: 7.6-cm-thick x 5.8-cm diameter)
and three of type EJ- 315M (size: 7.6-cm-thick x
5.8-cm diameter). Reference detector EJ-315 was
fixed at a distance of 27 cm from the target at 60
deg. The angular distribution was measured with other
four detectors placed at a distance 63 cm from the
target and rotated on a swing arm at laboratory
angular positions between 20 deg and 150 deg.
High purity Ge detector with efficiency of 54%
placed at an angle of 130deg and a distance of 22.5cm
was used to monitor secondary gamma rays from
10B(alpha,p'gamma) 13C reaction mainly for
troubleshooting during the experiment
Neutron spectra were determined by unfolding
the measured energy distribution.
Data normalized to data in REL-REF.
Compiled by S.H.
Data taken from Tab. 1 of the reference
Systematic uncertainty
Data uncertainty dominated by unfolding
uncertainty
